Gene_Accession_Number.reason: Accession numbers list ITPK1 as At5g16760.

Source_Species.reason: Paper studies Arabidopsis and specifies Arabidopsis thaliana.

Source_Species_Latin_Name.reason: Title/materials identify the species as Arabidopsis thaliana.

Applied_Promoters.reason: Complementation used a genomic ITPK1 fragment including a 1,839-bp upstream region of the ITPK1 start codon, i.e. native promoter context.

Protein_Family_or_Domain.reason: Gene name and title identify ITPK1 as INOSITOL (1,3,4) TRIPHOSPHATE 5/6 KINASE1, an inositol phosphate kinase.

Enzyme_Name.reason: Explicitly given in the title and abstract.

Biosynthetic_Pathway.reason: Results and abstract place ITPK1 in inositol polyphosphate / pyrophosphate metabolism.

Pathway_Branch_or_Subpathway.reason: Paper shows ITPK1 stimulates 5-InsP7 synthesis and affects broader InsP interconversions.

Metabolic_Step_Position.reason: ITPK1 acts on InsP6 to make InsP7 and also on lower InsPs, fitting a mid-to-downstream PP-InsP-forming step.

Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step.reason: Paper supports ITPK1 as important for 5-InsP7 homeostasis and auxin responses, but does not explicitly establish a rate-limiting step.

Primary_Substrate.reason: Abstract/background states ITPK1 has in vitro InsP6 kinase activity; Results show recombinant ITPK1 phosphorylates purified InsP4a.

Primary_Product.reason: Results show reduced 5-InsP7 in itpk1 and discuss ITPK1-dependent InsP/PP-InsP species; 5-InsP7 is directly supported.

Catalyzed_Reaction_Description.reason: Paper reports in vitro InsP6 kinase activity and reduced 5-InsP7 in planta in itpk1, plus phosphorylation of InsP4a by recombinant ITPK1.

End_Product_Connection_Type.reason: Results and abstract support that ITPK1-dependent InsP/PP-InsPs fine-tune auxin receptor complex/activity and auxin responses.

Terminal_Metabolite.reason: 5-InsP7 is supported, but the broader phrase 'inositol polyphosphates/pyrophosphates involved in auxin signaling' is somewhat generic and includes inferred species not definitively assigned.

Terminal_Metabolite_Function.reason: Paper links ITPK1-dependent InsP/PP-InsP status to auxin perception and auxin-related developmental responses including those listed.

Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site.reason: Metabolites were profiled mainly in seedlings/seedling extracts; shoots and roots were measured for auxin levels and gene expression, not for terminal InsP/PP-InsP accumulation separately.

Core_Phenotypic_Effect.reason: Loss of ITPK1 causes auxin-insensitive phenotypes and altered InsP homeostasis; complementation rescues defects.

Core_Validation_Method.reason: All listed approaches were used in Results for ITPK1 except 'receptor binding assay context' which is not direct gene perturbation but is part of the study context; overall statement is faithful.

Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species.reason: Figure 7A reports ubiquitous expression with high ITPK1 transcript levels in all tested tissues.

Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species.reason: Same Arabidopsis system; Figure 7A supports ubiquitous expression across tissues.

Subcellular_Localization.reason: BiFC shows ITPK1-TIR1 interaction signal in the nucleus, but this does not by itself establish full subcellular localization of ITPK1 generally.

Interacting_Proteins.reason: Co-IP and BiFC support physical interaction with TIR1.

Environment_or_Treatment_Factor.reason: Results used exogenous IAA, NAA, elevated temperature, gravitropic reorientation, and phosphate-replete/low-phosphate conditions.

Omics_Data.reason: Paper includes SAX-HPLC, CE-ESI-MS, and RT-qPCR; no public omics accession is reported.

Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene.reason: All three summary points are directly supported by Results and figures.

Potential_Tradeoffs.reason: Results discuss constitutive phosphate starvation responses and phosphate over-accumulation in itpk1 under Pi-sufficient conditions, largely independent of auxin phenotype.

Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu.reason: This is a forward-looking application inference not stated or tested in the paper.

Other_Important_Info.reason: Results explicitly state no detectable InsP6 change in itpk1, reduced 5-InsP7, and that 5-InsP7 or another ITPK1-dependent InsP/PP-InsP likely activates the receptor complex.

Gene_Accession_Number.reason: Accession numbers list ITPK2 as At4g33770.

Applied_Species.reason: Mutant analysis was performed in Arabidopsis.

Applied_Promoters.reason: The paper examines endogenous gene expression, but no promoter construct/use for ITPK2 is described.

Protein_Family_or_Domain.reason: ITPK2 is described as a homolog with InsP6-kinase activity similar to ITPK1; as named it belongs to the ITPK family.

Enzyme_Name.reason: The gene name corresponds to inositol (1,3,4) trisphosphate 5/6 kinase 2.

Biosynthetic_Pathway.reason: ITPK2 is discussed in the context of InsP/PP-InsP homeostasis.

Pathway_Branch_or_Subpathway.reason: Paper cites in vitro InsP6-kinase activity similar to ITPK1, placing it in the InsP6-to-PP-InsP branch.

Metabolic_Step_Position.reason: As an InsP6 kinase homolog acting redundantly with ITPK1, this mid/downstream placement is consistent with the paper.

Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step.reason: Paper shows it is not established alone but acts redundantly with ITPK1 for auxin responses.

Primary_Substrate.reason: Discussion of prior work says ITPK2 displays InsP6 kinase activity in vitro similar to ITPK1.

Primary_Product.reason: The paper supports InsP6 kinase activity in vitro but does not explicitly state the exact product here; 'likely 5-InsP7' is inferred from prior work rather than directly shown in this paper.

Catalyzed_Reaction_Description.reason: Results explicitly cite ITPK2 InsP6 kinase activity in vitro and show redundant contribution to auxin responses.

End_Product_Connection_Type.reason: Redundant contribution to auxin responses through InsP/PP-InsP metabolism is supported by the double-mutant data.

Terminal_Metabolite.reason: Only generic auxin-linked inositol pyrophosphates can be inferred for ITPK2 from redundancy; no specific metabolite contribution was demonstrated in planta for single mutant.

Terminal_Metabolite_Class.reason: InsP/PP-InsP class is clearly the relevant metabolite class.

Terminal_Metabolite_Function.reason: Double mutant data support a role in normal auxin-related root hair and gravitropic responses redundantly with ITPK1.

Core_Phenotypic_Effect.reason: Single mutant lacked obvious defects, while double mutant enhanced auxin-related defects; this redundancy claim is directly supported.

Core_Validation_Method.reason: RT-qPCR and single/double mutant phenotyping were done; prior in vitro activity and earlier CE-ESI-MS are cited as context.

Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species.reason: Figure 7A shows broad expression with especially strong transcript levels in root and hypocotyl.

Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species.reason: Same as above in the experimental Arabidopsis material.

Environment_or_Treatment_Factor.reason: IAA treatment and gravitropic reorientation were used for auxin response assays.

Omics_Data.reason: SAX-HPLC and cited CE-ESI-MS context plus RT-qPCR are present; no public accession reported.

Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene.reason: All three listed findings are directly consistent with Figure 7 and related text.

Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu.reason: Application value is speculative and not addressed in the paper.

Other_Important_Info.reason: Paper explicitly notes no obvious in planta InsP/PP-InsP defect in itpk2-2 under Pi-replete conditions despite in vitro kinase activity.

Gene_Accession_Number.reason: Accession numbers list IPK1 as At5g42810.

Applied_Species.reason: ipk1-1 mutant analyzed is in Arabidopsis.

Applied_Species_Variety.reason: All mutants are in Col-0 background.

Applied_Promoters.reason: No promoter construct or promoter usage for IPK1 is described in this paper.

Protein_Family_or_Domain.reason: Results describe IPK1 as the InsP5[2-OH] kinase.

Enzyme_Name.reason: IPK1 is identified as an inositol pentakisphosphate 2-kinase.

Biosynthetic_Pathway.reason: IPK1 is discussed in InsP6/phytic acid and downstream PP-InsP biosynthesis.

Pathway_Branch_or_Subpathway.reason: Paper explicitly states ipk1 mutants are compromised in conversion of InsP5[2-OH] to InsP6.

Metabolic_Step_Position.reason: This downstream step producing InsP6 as precursor for PP-InsPs is directly supported.

Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step.reason: Importance for InsP6 and downstream PP-InsP homeostasis is supported, but 'key step' is interpretive and 'not explicitly rate-limiting' is not a paper statement.

Primary_Substrate.reason: Results explicitly mention InsP5 [2-OH] as IPK1 substrate.

Primary_Product.reason: Results explicitly say IPK1 converts InsP5 [2-OH] to InsP6.

Catalyzed_Reaction_Description.reason: This reaction and its consequence for InsP7/InsP8 levels are directly described.

End_Product_Connection_Type.reason: Paper links IPK1-derived InsP6 pool to auxin-relevant PP-InsPs and auxin perception.

Terminal_Metabolite.reason: The paper implicates decreased 5-InsP7 and/or related InsPs in auxin signaling, but does not definitively assign a single terminal metabolite for IPK1.

Terminal_Metabolite_Function.reason: Results and discussion connect proper InsP6/PP-InsP homeostasis with auxin perception.

Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site.reason: The text mentions vegetative tissues for some contexts and seedlings for profiling, but does not explicitly define a specific IPK1-related terminal metabolite accumulation site as stated.

Core_Phenotypic_Effect.reason: Supplemental Figure S7 is described as showing decreased auxin responses in ipk1 plants.

Core_Validation_Method.reason: Mutant phenotyping and CE-ESI-MS profiling are in Results; biochemical assignment is cited from prior work.

Environment_or_Treatment_Factor.reason: Auxin-related assays with exogenous IAA are described for ipk1 in supplemental results.

Omics_Data.reason: CE-ESI-MS metabolite profiling is explicitly described; no public accession is given.

Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene.reason: All three points are consistent with the Results text on ipk1 and Supplemental Figure S7.

Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu.reason: This is speculative and not tested or discussed as an application in the paper.

Other_Important_Info.reason: Results directly tested ipk1 auxin phenotypes and metabolite changes, so inclusion as a directly tested pathway gene is justified.

corrections.reason: No promoter construct or promoter usage for IPK1 is described in this paper.

Gene_Accession_Number.reason: Accession numbers list TIR1 as At3g62980.

Source_Species.reason: TIR1 is from Arabidopsis in this study.

Source_Species_Variety.reason: The study background is Col-0 and tir1 allele is Arabidopsis.

Applied_Species.reason: TIR1 was analyzed in Arabidopsis and also used in heterologous BiFC assays.

Applied_Species_Latin_Name.reason: Primary source gene is Arabidopsis thaliana.

Applied_Species_Variety.reason: TIR1 constructs were from wild-type Col-0 cDNA, but recombinant ASK1-TIR1 also came from insect cells; variety is not always relevant/explicit in those assays.

Protein_Family_or_Domain.reason: Abstract/results identify TIR1 as an F-box protein; discussion references LRR solenoid architecture.

Regulator_Type.reason: TIR1 is the auxin receptor F-box regulatory protein.

Metabolic_Process_Controlled.reason: TIR1 controls auxin perception/signaling, but calling this a 'metabolic process' is imprecise; modulation by InsPs is supported.

Regulation_Mode.reason: In vitro assays show auxin-dependent ASK1-TIR1-IAA7 complex formation and binding of inositol polyphosphates.

Primary_Regulatory_Targets.reason: IAA7 is directly used in assays; auxin-responsive gene expression program is the established output discussed in paper.

Regulatory_Effect_on_Target_Genes.reason: Discussion describes degradation of Aux/IAA repressors enabling auxin-responsive gene expression.

Decisive_Influence_on_Target_Product.reason: Results/abstract propose TIR1-containing receptor activity is fine-tuned by 5-InsP7 or another ITPK1-dependent InsP/PP-InsP.

Upstream_Signals_or_Inputs.reason: IAA, ASK1 association, and ITPK1-dependent inositol polyphosphates are all directly discussed.

Protein_Complex_Involvement.reason: Paper repeatedly analyzes the ASK1-TIR1-IAA7 auxin receptor complex.

Interacting_Proteins.reason: ASK1 and IAA7 are part of the receptor complex assay; ITPK1 interaction with TIR1 is shown by co-IP and BiFC.

Subcellular_Localization.reason: BiFC signal for ITPK1-TIR1 interaction is detected in the nucleus.

Environment_or_Treatment_Factor.reason: IAA is used in binding assays and is the relevant treatment/input.

Applied_Promoters.reason: No promoter information or promoter construct for TIR1 is described.

Omics_Data.reason: Radioligand binding, co-IP, and BiFC are indeed used; no accession reported.

Terminal_Metabolite.reason: The paper supports binding of InsP6 and 5-InsP7 and refers more generally to ITPK1-dependent InsP/PP-InsPs, but not a single defined terminal metabolite.

Terminal_Metabolite_Function.reason: Paper supports fine-tuning of auxin perception and developmental responses by these ligands.

Core_Phenotypic_Effect.reason: Results show high-affinity binding of InsP6 and 5-InsP7 to receptor complex and propose activation/fine-tuning of auxin responsiveness.

Core_Validation_Method.reason: All three listed methods are explicitly used in Results.

Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene.reason: All three summary points are directly supported by Figures 5 and 6 and corresponding text.

Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu.reason: This engineering implication is speculative and not evaluated in the paper.

Other_Important_Info.reason: Discussion cites Supplemental Figure S11B showing TIR1 stability not compromised in itpk1; interpretation as activation-state rather than abundance is consistent with paper.

corrections.reason: No promoter information or promoter construct for TIR1 is described.
